title: Poppy seed and lemon cold-process soap
subtitle: A gentle exfoliating bar with poppy seeds and lemon essential oil
excerpt: Poppy seeds stirred into the batter at trace give this bar a fine, even exfoliating texture. Lemon essential oil brightens the fragrance at pour but fades to a very light citrus note through the cure; add lemon myrtle if a stronger citrus note through cure is wanted.
sourceNotes: Exfoliant application in cold-process soap from Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). SAP values from SoapCalc. Lemon EO phototoxicity note from Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter (~7 x 130 g bars after 4-week cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Recalculate lye through a soap calculator before making.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for a minimum of 4 weeks. The lemon scent in the cured bar will be very light; this is normal for citrus essential oils in cold-process soap.
recipe.temperatureNote: Combine lye solution and oils when both are between 38 and 42 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years from cure date in a cool, dry place. The poppy seeds do not shorten shelf life. Discard if the bar smells rancid or shows dreaded orange spots.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oils left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5%, it adds skin conditioning without making the bar feel greasy.

The point where the blended oils and lye solution have emulsified enough to leave a visible trail when drizzled. Add poppy seeds and essential oil at light trace so they disperse evenly through the batter.

The reaction between sodium hydroxide and the oils that produces soap and glycerol. Citrus essential oils are volatile; a significant portion evaporates during saponification, which is why the lemon scent fades after the cure.

content.text: Poppy seeds used in soap making are the same culinary poppy seeds sold in supermarkets. They are small enough to provide gentle exfoliation without scratching, and their blue-grey colour gives the finished bar a speckled look. Stir them in at
rather than adding them to the oils or lye solution; adding them too early means they settle before the batter thickens.
Cold-pressed lemon essential oil is phototoxic, but

breaks down the bergapten compounds that cause this, making rinse-off soap safe. The volatile citrus molecules also evaporate during the cure, leaving only a faint lemon note. For a stronger citrus scent, add lemon myrtle: it survives the cure better. This formula uses a 5%
This recipe uses sodium hydroxide. Follow the
guidance before starting. Wear protective goggles and gloves throughout.

items.prepNote: 35% of oil weight; melt if solid
Run through a lye calculator to confirm
3% of oil weight; added at light trace

content.text: technique. Weigh, melt, and cool all oils to 38 to 42 degrees Celsius. Mix the lye solution and cool to the same range. Pour the lye solution into the oils and blend to a light trace. Stir in the poppy seeds and lemon essential oil by hand until evenly distributed. Pour into the mould, smooth the top, and leave undisturbed for 48 hours. Unmould, cut, and cure on a rack for a minimum of 4 weeks.

Cured bars keep for 2 years in a cool, dry place on a rack. Poppy seeds are inert once embedded in soap and do not affect shelf life. Discard any bar that smells rancid or shows orange or brown spots.
